

# **Abstract**

The massive growth in 5G/6G communications, IoT, and cloud applications has created network traffic characterized by unprecedented scale, dynamism, and heterogeneity. Traditional, tightly-coupled network architectures with distributed routing protocols and fragmented state views lack the agility to manage this volatile environment, leading to inefficiencies. Software-Defined Networking (SDN) emerged to solve this by decoupling the control and data planes, centralizing network intelligence, and enabling global, dynamic programmability. However, optimizing routing for millions of concurrent flows in SDN is an NP-hard problem. Deterministic traffic engineering algorithms, such as static heuristics or constrained shortest-path calculations, quickly degrade under modern, non-stationary traffic, causing congestion, high latency, and poor Quality of Service (QoS). To overcome the limitations of these static methods, integrating Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) into SDN is proposed as the most viable path for autonomous traffic engineering. This proposal outlines the architectural design for dynamic SDN traffic routing using DRL, reviewing the evolution from tabular Q-learning to advanced actor-critic models, the incorporation of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for topology generalization, and the role of Agentic AI. The analysis culminates in the proposition of the Graph-Attention Reinforcement Routing Orchestrator (GARRO) to achieve adaptive, self-healing, and QoS-aware routing.

**Keywords:** Software-Defined Networking, Deep Reinforcement Learning, Proximal Policy Optimization, Graph Transformers, Traffic Engineering, Digital Twin, Agentic AI, QoS

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# **1.0 Introduction**

Traditional routing in IP networks is largely governed by interior gateway protocols such as OSPF and IS-IS, which compute forwarding paths using variants of Dijkstra’s shortest-path algorithm based on predefined link weights. While highly scalable and effective in stable environments, shortest-path routing consistently routes traffic through a singular optimal path regardless of current bandwidth utilization or real-time network conditions (*Abrol et al., 2024*). Consequently, central links frequently become congested bottlenecks while peripheral links remain underutilized, severely degrading network throughput, increasing transmission delay, and elevating packet loss rates.

To introduce load distribution, Equal-Cost Multi-Path (ECMP) routing was widely adopted. ECMP splits traffic flows across multiple paths of equal cost using hash-based load balancing. While ECMP improves upon singular shortest-path routing, it remains fundamentally blind to real-time network states and does not dynamically adjust to transient microbursts or asymmetrical traffic loads, leading to hash collisions where multiple heavy flows (often referred to as "elephant flows") are mapped to the same path, overwhelming link capacity and causing queue overflows (*Kim et al., 2022*).

Recent iterations of traffic engineering have attempted to dynamically adjust OSPF link weights or deploy Segment Routing over IPv6 (SRv6) to direct traffic explicitly. However, dynamically recalculating and disseminating link weights in a distributed architecture induces severe signaling overhead and route flapping, while Segment Routing introduces significant header overhead and scalability demands (*Casas-Velasco et al., 2021*). Software-Defined Wide Area Networking (SD-WAN) provides cost-effective flexibility but struggles to deliver the strict, deterministic QoS guarantees required by 5G and 6G applications (*Casas-Velasco et al., 2021*).

Deep Reinforcement Learning circumvents the limitations of both heuristics and supervised learning through an experience-driven, model-free architecture. A DRL agent operates as an active decision-maker situated within the SDN controller, or as a dedicated application communicating via northbound APIs. By observing the network state, taking routing actions, and receiving performance-based rewards, the DRL agent approximates complex, non-linear control policies without requiring prior labeled datasets (*Abrol et al., 2024*). The efficacy of the traffic engineering system depends heavily on the specific reinforcement learning algorithm deployed, with the evolution of DRL algorithms from 2020 to 2026 highlighting a shift toward architectures that handle high-dimensional continuous spaces, ensure stable convergence, and adapt to topological mutations.

## **1.1 Problem Statement**

Even within an SDN environment, centralized heuristic algorithms scale poorly. As the number of nodes and links increases, the computational time required to solve linear programming models for traffic matrices grows exponentially, making real-time, microsecond-level flow scheduling mathematically intractable (*Abrol et al., 2024*). Supervised deep learning models have been proposed to predict traffic matrices and aid in routing classification, yet such models rely entirely on massive datasets of accurately labeled data. In network environments, obtaining labeled data that maps specific traffic states to optimal routing configurations is nearly impossible due to the infinite permutation of network states, traffic matrices, and microbursts. Furthermore, supervised models suffer from severe generalization failures commonly referred to as the "reality gap" when deployed in environments with traffic distributions differing from their training data (*Abrol et al., 2024*).

Initial intelligent routing frameworks utilized classical tabular Q-Learning. By maintaining a Q-table storing the expected utility of every state-action pair, agents could route traffic dynamically. The Q-Optimizer framework, refined as recently as 2026, utilizes lightweight, two-stage tabular learning relying on offline pre-evaluation and real-time rule-based adaptation, demonstrating improvements of 36.49% in throughput, 46.09% in round-trip time (RTT) reduction, and 95.01% in jitter minimization compared to Dijkstra's algorithm in constrained topologies (*Goteti & Reddy, 2026*). However, as the network scales, state-action permutations trigger the "curse of dimensionality," rendering tabular Q-tables unmanageable for large-scale enterprise or carrier networks (*Abrol et al., 2024*).

To solve the dimensionality constraint, Deep Q-Networks (DQN) employ Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) as function approximators for the Q-table. DQN-based frameworks such as the Deep Reinforcement Learning and Software-Defined Networking Intelligent Routing (DRSIR) framework have proven highly successful, employing an off-policy approach with Online and Target Neural Networks and Experience Replay Memory to break correlation between consecutive network states. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that DQN agents reduce traffic delay by up to 16.1% and increase throughput by 7.8% compared to OSPF (*Casas-Velasco et al., 2021*). Despite these improvements, basic DQN is restricted to discrete action spaces and is notorious for overestimating action values. Double DQN (DDQN) mitigates this overestimation bias by decoupling action selection from action evaluation, achieving the highest throughput across fluctuating traffic episodes and reflecting superior convergence stability (*Zhang et al., 2024*).

When the routing formulation requires continuous action spaces—such as dynamically assigning continuous link weights across an entire topology—the Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) algorithm utilizes an Actor-Critic architecture. The Actor network outputs continuous deterministic actions while the Critic network evaluates the action-value function (*Abrol et al., 2024*). While DDPG allows for fine-grained traffic engineering, it suffers from severe training instability, overestimation of Q-values, and extreme sensitivity to hyperparameter tuning (*Abrol et al., 2024*). A fundamental barrier to deploying DRL in production SDNs is the performance degradation during the initial exploration phase, where untrained agents will inevitably select suboptimal or catastrophic routing paths resulting in severe packet loss and intolerable latency.

## **1.2 Aim and Objectives of the Study**

The primary aim of this study is to design, develop, and evaluate a hybrid intelligent routing architecture, termed the Graph-Attention Reinforcement Routing Orchestrator (GARRO), tailored for dynamic Software-Defined Networks.

To achieve this aim, the study will pursue the following specific objectives:

1. Formulate the dynamic traffic routing problem as a multi-objective Markov Decision Process (MDP) that balances throughput maximization, delay minimization, and load distribution.

2. Develop a decision engine based on Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) combined with Graph Transformers to process graph-structured network telemetry and execute topology-agnostic routing decisions.

3. Design and implement a decoupled offline Digital Twin training environment using M/M/1/K queuing theory, preventing live-network Quality of Service (QoS) degradation during the agent's exploration phase.

4. Integrate an Agentic AI supervisory layer utilizing Large Language Models (LLMs) to translate high-level semantic operational intents into DRL reward weights and provide deterministic fallback routing for absolute fault tolerance.

5. Rigorously benchmark the proposed GARRO architecture against traditional protocols (OSPF, ECMP) and baseline DRL methods (DQN, DDPG) across diverse topologies (NSFNET, GEANT2, Fat-Tree) using dynamic traffic matrices.

## **1.3 Methodology**

### ***Multi-Tiered Plane Architecture***

The proposed GARRO framework operates across three highly integrated planes (*Goteti & Reddy, 2026*):

1. **Data Plane (Forwarding Plane):** Comprises OpenFlow-enabled switches generating continuous telemetry, including port statistics, byte counters, and queue depths.

2. **Control Plane:** Utilizes a distributed SDN controller cluster (e.g., ONOS or OS-Ken) to execute flow installations, monitor topology via LLDP, and enforce routing rules.

3. **AI Decision Plane (Knowledge Plane):** Hosts the DRL agents, the offline simulation engines, and the Agentic LLM orchestrator.

![][image1]

### **Figure 1.1: System Flow Diagram of the Proposed GARRO Architecture**

### 

### 

### 

### ***Theoretical Framework and MDP Formulation***

The dynamic traffic routing problem is mathematically formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) defined by the tuple (S, A, R, P, γ), where S is the state space, A is the action space, R is the reward function, P is the transition probability, and γ is the discount factor (*Abrol et al., 2024*).

**Advanced State Space Encoding (S):** The state space encapsulates a high-fidelity representation of the network's current operational status through the Network State Matrix (NSM), integrating link-level and node-level features extracted via OpenFlow telemetry and LLDP polling (*Abrol et al., 2024*). Link state includes a normalized Network Adjacency Matrix with instantaneous throughput, maximum capacity, and link latency. Node state captures node degree, centrality, aggregated ingress/egress rates, and buffer occupancy. The Aggregated Traffic Volume Matrix (ATVM) represents traffic volume processed by each intermediate switch, providing precise localization of traffic bottlenecks (*Abrol et al., 2024*).

**Strategic Action Space Modeling (A):** The action space is formulated as end-to-end path selection. The agent selects an optimal path from a predefined set of K-shortest candidate paths for a specific source-destination pair (*Abrol et al., 2024; Kim et al., 2022*). This discrete action space significantly reduces complexity and prevents routing loops. Continuous link weight assignment, while theoretically compatible with legacy OSPF protocols, increases training instability and exacerbates controller overhead (*Zhang et al., 2024*).

**Multi-Objective Reward Function (R):** The reward function mathematically quantifies network QoS and penalizes congestion. For a given action aₜ at state sₜ, the immediate reward rₜ is a composite function (*Abrol et al., 2024; Goteti & Reddy, 2026*):rₜ \= α₁(T\_actual/T\_requested) \- α₂(D\_path) \- α₃(L\_packet) \- α₄(Var\_util)where T\_actual is the actual throughput achieved, T\_requested is the requested throughput, D\_path is the end-to-end path delay, L\_packet is the packet loss ratio, and Var\_util is the variance in link utilization across the network. The coefficients α₁ through α₄ are tunable hyperparameters that weight the relative importance of each metric, dynamically adjustable based on specific network slicing requirements.

### ***State Encoding via Graph Transformers***

To ensure resilience against topology modifications, the network state is processed as a graph-structured input rather than a flat matrix. The SDN controller feeds telemetry into a Graph Transformer network (*Chen et al., 2024*). Each node nᵢ is assigned a feature vector containing aggregated ingress/egress bytes, CPU load, and buffer occupancy, while each edge eᵢⱼ contains link capacity, instantaneous throughput, and delay. The Graph Transformer utilizes multi-head self-attention incorporating a virtual star node to instantly pass messages across the entire network diameter, generating a low-dimensional, topology-agnostic latent representation of the global network state. This ensures the model does not require retraining if a switch fails or a link is added (*Chen et al., 2024*).

### ***PPO-Based Decision Engine***

The latent state vector is ingested by a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent. PPO is selected over DQN and DDPG due to its superior stability, monotonic policy improvement capabilities, and fast convergence (*Abrol et al., 2024*). The action space is formulated as K-shortest path selection, where the PPO actor network outputs a probability distribution over K viable paths, selecting the path with the highest probability. PPO achieves the fastest convergence, highest learning stability, and the lowest overall average flow latency in complex Fat-Tree data center topologies, significantly outperforming both DQN and DDPG in dynamic SDN routing tasks (*Abrol et al., 2024*).

### ***Zero-Impact Training via Digital Twin***

To bridge the reality gap and eliminate live-network degradation, GARRO employs an offline Digital Twin utilizing M/M/1/K queuing calculus and historical traffic matrices (*Abrol et al., 2024*). The network's data processing and transmission delays are modeled stochastically, with Poisson arrivals at rate λ and exponentially distributed service times μ. The expected queue occupation E\[Q\] and the probability of buffer overflow P(overflow) at each switch sᵢ with capacity K are calculated continuously. The PPO agent trains extensively within this simulated environment, utilizing Priority Experience Replay to learn from rare but catastrophic congestion events. Policy updates are transferred to the live SDN controller only after mathematical convergence is verified.

### ***Implementation Strategy and Experimental Setup***

The experimental testbed will be constructed using Mininet to emulate the data plane, interacting via OpenFlow 1.3 with a distributed ONOS or OS-Ken controller cluster (*Goteti & Reddy, 2026*). The AI Decision Plane will be developed in Python utilizing PyTorch or TensorFlow for the Graph Transformer and PPO implementations, with Ray RLlib handling multi-agent distributed training and orchestration.

Testing will span diverse topologies to verify generalization and scalability:

1. **NSFNET (14 nodes, 21 links):** A standard wide-area network (WAN) backbone model.  
2. **GEANT2 (24 nodes, 37 links):** A highly irregular European academic network used to test edge-centrality awareness and load balancing under asymmetrical constraints.  
3. **Fat-Tree (k=8):** A dense data center network topology to evaluate multi-path collision avoidance, scale, and performance under massive East-West traffic flows.

Traffic will be generated using iperf3 and the Modulated Gravity Model (TMGen) to simulate highly bursty, non-stationary traffic matrices typical of IoT and multimedia workloads, with the coefficient of variance for traffic arrival strictly manipulated to emulate microbursts.

## **1.4 Expected Contribution to Knowledge**

The proposed study contributes to the growing body of knowledge on intelligent Software-Defined Networking (SDN) by integrating advanced Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL), Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), metaheuristic optimization techniques, and Agentic AI. Existing research demonstrates that Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) provides superior routing performance compared to traditional DRL algorithms such as DQN and DDPG, offering improved convergence speed, learning stability, and reduced network latency (Abrol et al., 2024). By leveraging PPO, the proposed framework aims to achieve more efficient and adaptive routing decisions in dynamic network environments.

The study further contributes by utilizing Graph Neural Networks to represent network topologies. Unlike conventional neural network architectures that require retraining when network structures change, GNNs can naturally process graph-based network data and adapt to topology variations such as link failures and node additions (Chen et al., 2024; Abrol et al., 2024). This enhances scalability, resilience, and the ability of the routing system to operate effectively in evolving network conditions.

To improve optimization efficiency and avoid local optima, the framework incorporates metaheuristic techniques inspired by the African Vulture Optimization Algorithm (AVOA). This approach enhances global search capabilities and enables more balanced traffic distribution by identifying and mitigating network bottlenecks (Chen et al., 2024). As a result, the proposed solution is expected to provide superior load balancing and resource utilization compared to conventional routing approaches.

Additionally, the integration of Agentic AI and Large Language Models (LLMs) introduces semantic reasoning and intent-awareness into network management. While DRL handles real-time routing optimization, LLMs can interpret high-level operator objectives and translate them into network policies, thereby improving decision transparency and supporting intent-based networking (Cui et al., 2025; Goteti & Reddy, 2026). The framework also incorporates self-healing capabilities, enabling automatic fault detection, route recomputation, and rapid recovery from network failures while minimizing control-plane overhead (Bholani, 2026).

Overall, this research proposes a comprehensive and future-ready SDN routing architecture capable of supporting emerging networking paradigms such as 6G networks, Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTNs), Vehicular Ad Hoc Networks (VANETs), network slicing, and service function chaining. The study is expected to advance the state of intelligent network management by delivering a routing solution that is adaptive, scalable, resilient, and capable of meeting the demands of next-generation communication networks.

## 

## **1.5 Conclusion**

The shift from rule-based traffic engineering to AI-driven autonomous routing marks a fundamental change in network management, especially for complex environments like 5G, 6G, and large-scale cloud systems. While early deep reinforcement learning approaches showed that intelligent, model-free routing is possible, they fall short in scalability, flexibility, and stability.

Modern solutions require hybrid architectures, such as GARRO, which combines stable learning methods with graph-based models to handle dynamic network topologies without constant retraining. By incorporating digital twin environments for safe offline training and an agentic AI layer to align system behavior with human intent, this approach addresses past limitations and improves reliability.

Although deploying such systems demands significant investment in infrastructure and processing capabilities, the expected gains in performance, efficiency, and self-recovery make it a practical path toward building resilient, future-ready networks.

# **2.0 Literature Review**

## **2.1 Conceptual Foundations of SDN and Traffic Engineering**

Traditional telecommunication networks rely on tightly integrated control and data planes, where decentralized routers running protocols such as Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) or Intermediate System to Intermediate System (IS-IS) make autonomous forwarding decisions. While highly resilient, this architecture suffers from a lack of global visibility, making optimal traffic engineering (TE) mathematically difficult to achieve under dynamic, bursty, and non-stationary traffic demands.

The introduction of Software-Defined Networking (SDN) fundamentally decoupled these concerns, dividing the network into functional layers as shown in Figure 2.1:

![][image2]

*Figure 2.1: Functional Planes of the Software-Defined Networking Architecture (Adapted from ONF, 2014).*

### 

### ***2.1.1 The Architecture of Software-Defined Networking (SDN)***

In an SDN architecture, the control plane is centralized in a software-based entity (such as ONOS or OS-Ken), leaving the data plane to perform pure, hardware-accelerated packet forwarding based on rules pushed by the controller (Al-Sadi & Al-Anbaki, 2025). Southbound APIs, primarily OpenFlow and P4, standardize the communication between the control and data planes, allowing the controller to query port statistics, configure flow entries, and monitor link states via Link Layer Discovery Protocol (LLDP) frames.

Centralization provides a global, high-fidelity view of the network's topology and traffic state. However, this architectural shift shifts the bottleneck from the distributed consensus protocols of traditional networks to the computational capability of the SDN controller. Consequently, any dynamic routing orchestrator operating at the control plane must execute pathway computations with minimal control-plane latency to prevent queue build-up and flow rule installation delays.

### ***2.1.2 Traditional vs. SDN-Based Traffic Engineering (TE)***

Traffic Engineering aims to optimize network performance by dynamically mapping traffic flows to physical topologies to maximize resource utilization and satisfy Quality of Service (QoS) constraints. Table 2.1 summarizes the architectural differences between traditional, heuristic, and SDN-based intelligent routing.

| Metric / Dimension | Traditional Shortest Path (OSPF / IS-IS) | Equal-Cost Multi-Path (ECMP) | SDN-Based Intelligent Routing (GARRO) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Topology View** | Distributed (Link-State Advertisements) | Distributed / Local hash-based splitting | Centralized, high-fidelity global view |
| **Path Selection** | Static single shortest path (Dijkstra) | Static multi-path splitting (equal metrics) | Dynamic, adaptive path selection per flow |
| **Congestion Awareness** | Reactive (manual link weight adjustments) | Blind to real-time link queue build-ups | Proactive and real-time state adaptive |
| **Overhead** | High distributed convergence signalling | Low (hash calculations) | High controller polling (mitigated by AI) |
| **Scalability** | High, robust to single node failures | High, but prone to elephant flow collisions | Extremely high when generalized via GNNs |

*Table 2.1: Comparative Analysis of Routing Paradigms.*

As shown in Table 2.1, heuristic protocols such as OSPF prioritize path minimization over bandwidth optimization, driving central links into saturation while edge links sit idle (Abrol et al., 2024). Equal-Cost Multi-Pathing (ECMP) attempts to alleviate this by splitting flows across paths of equal routing cost using a hash of the packet header (5-tuple). However, because ECMP does not measure real-time link utilization, it is vulnerable to "hash collisions." This occurs when multiple highly active flows ("elephant flows") map to the same path, overwhelming interface buffers and generating microburst-induced packet drops while alternative parallel paths remain empty (Kim et al., 2022).

## **2.2 Fundamentals of Reinforcement Learning in Networks**

Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) reformulates traffic routing from a static optimization problem into a dynamic, experience-driven control loop. Unlike supervised learning, which requires a pre-labeled dataset mapping traffic matrices to optimal paths—a task that is often mathematically intractable due to the high-dimensional permutations of flow characteristics—DRL learns an optimal control policy through continuous, active interaction with the network environment.

### ***2.2.1 The Markov Decision Process (MDP) Formulation***

To apply DRL to dynamic routing, the control loop must be mathematically formalized as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), characterized by the tuple (S,A,P,R,γ).

#### *1\. State Space (s)*

The state space must provide a high-fidelity representation of the network's current operational status while remaining computationally efficient to process. It is represented as a network graph G=(V,E), where V is the set of switch nodes and E is the set of physical communication links. The global state st∈S  at time t is represented as a structured matrix embedding both node-level features vi∈V and link-level features eij∈E:

st= ( { hij}i νVt, { hij}ij ∈Et)  
Where:

* hvit= Cvi, Bvi, Rin,vi, Rout,vi contains the CPU utilization Cvi, buffer occupancy Bvi, and aggregated ingress/egress data rates Rin, Rout of switch vi.  
* h\_{ij}^{t} \= \[BW\_{max,ij}, BW\_{util,ij}, D\_{ij}, L\_{ij}\] represents the physical bandwidth capacity BW\_{max}, real-time bandwidth utilization BW\_{util}, measured propagation and queuing delay D\_{ij}, and packet loss rate L\_{ij} of link e\_{ij}.

#### *2\. Action Space (A)*

The action space defines the set of routing decisions available to the controller. While early works mapped actions to continuous, link-weight modifications across the entire network topology, this approach induces high training instability and control-plane signaling overhead due to constant route flapping. Modern architectures model the action space as a discrete path selection problem from a set of candidate paths (Abrol et al., 2024):

A \= {p\_1, p\_2, ..., p\_K} Where {p\_k} represents the K\-shortest paths computed for a specific source-destination pair (s,d). The agent's action a\_t ∈ A is the selection of path p\_selected over which the target traffic flow is to be routed.

#### *3\. Reward Function (R)*

The reward function R(s\_t,a\_t) guides the agent toward policy convergence by mathematically formalizing the multi-objective goals of traffic engineering: throughput maximization, delay minimization, and load balancing (Goteti & Reddy, 2026). It is structured as:

R\_t \= α\_1(T\_actual/T\_requested) \- α\_2D\_path \- α\_3L\_packet \- α\_4σ\_util^2 Where:

* T\_actual and T\_requested are the achieved and demanded flow throughputs, respectively.  
* D\_path \= ∑\_{e∈p\_selected} D\_e is the cumulative end-to-end path delay.  
* L\_packet is the end-to-end packet loss ratio along the selected path.  
* σ\_util^2 is the variance of link utilization across the entire topology, which penalizes unbalanced loads.  
* α\_1, α\_2, α\_3, α\_4 ∈ \[0,1\] are tuning hyperparameters dynamically adjusted to align with active network slice Service Level Agreements (SLAs).

#### *4\. Transition Probability (P) and Discount Factor ()*

The state transition probability P(s\_{t+1}|s\_t,a\_t) models the stochastic evolution of the network state as traffic demands dynamically fluctuate. The discount factor γ ∈ \[0,1\] balances immediate reward feedback with long-term cumulative routing efficiency.

### ***2.2.2 Value-Based vs. Policy-Based DRL in Routing***

The operational mechanics of DRL algorithms determine their suitability for live-network deployment. Table 2.2 analyzes the theoretical and practical trade-offs between value-based, deterministic policy gradient, and stochastic policy gradient architectures when applied to SDN-based traffic engineering.

| Algorithm | Action Space Capability | Convergence Stability | Core Advantages in Networking | Core Disadvantages in Networking |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Deep Q-Network (DQN)** | Discrete Only | Low to Moderate | High sample efficiency via off-policy Experience Replay; simple action selection | Overestimates action values; scales poorly with state-action space dimensionality |
| **Double DQN (DDQN)** | Discrete Only | Moderate | Mitigates overestimation bias by decoupling action selection from value evaluation | Slow to adapt to sudden, highly dynamic traffic microbursts |
| **Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG)** | Continuous Only | Low | Permits continuous link-weight optimization and fine-grained resource tuning | Highly sensitive to hyperparameters; prone to severe divergence and training loops |
| **Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)** | Discrete & Continuous | High | Clipped objective prevents catastrophic routing policies; monotonic improvement | Marginally lower sample efficiency compared to off-policy counterparts |

*Table 2.2: Algorithmic Trade-offs in DRL-Based Routing Orchestration.*

As illustrated in Table 2.2, while value-based methods (DQN/DDQN) are suited for simple routing tasks, their off-policy nature and reliance on state-action value maximization (Q(s,a)) lead to slow convergence in high-dimensional topologies. Conversely, policy-based approaches optimize the policy parameters  directly to maximize the expected cumulative reward J(θ)=E\[∑\_t γ^tR\_t\].

Among these, Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) stands out for its deployment of a clipped surrogate objective function that prevents policy updates from changing too rapidly, making it well-suited for stable routing applications (Abrol et al., 2024).

## 

## 

## 

## **2.3 Evolution of Routing Optimization in SDN: A Systematic Review**

The trajectory of intelligent routing research has progressed through three primary epochs: classical heuristic/metaheuristic optimization, tabular reinforcement learning, and high-dimensional deep reinforcement learning.

### ***2.3.1 Tabular and Classical Heuristic Approaches***

Early attempts to optimize SDN routing moved away from static shortest-path heuristics toward metaheuristic algorithms and tabular reinforcement learning. Chen et al. (2024) developed the African Vulture Routing Optimization (AVRO) framework, based on the metaheuristic African Vulture Optimization Algorithm (AVOA). AVRO computes edge betweenness centrality:

C\_B(e) \= ∑\_{s≠t} σ\_st(e)/σ\_st Where σ\_st is the total number of shortest paths from node s to node t, and σ\_st(e) is the subset of those paths that traverse link e. By proactively identifying high-centrality core bottleneck links, AVRO computes alternative paths that avoid central congestion. Although AVRO demonstrated a 16.9% improvement over standard DRL models, its relies on iterative population-based search. This introduces a computational delay that scales with topology size, making it less suitable for real-time, millisecond-level traffic scheduling.

To reduce computational overhead, Goteti and Reddy (2026) introduced the Q-Optimizer framework, which uses a lightweight, tabular Q-Learning algorithm. Tabular Q-learning updates an explicit lookup table of Q(s,a) values based on the temporal difference (TD) error:

Q(s\_t,a\_t) ← Q(s\_t,a\_t) \+ β\[R\_{t+1} \+ γ max\_a Q(s\_{t+1},a) \- Q(s\_t,a\_t)\] Where β ∈ \[0,1\] is the learning rate. Q-Optimizer implemented a two-stage approach: offline lookup-table pre-evaluation paired with real-time rule adaptation. While it achieved a 36.49% throughput increase and a 46.09% RTT reduction over Dijkstra's algorithm in stable topologies, tabular methods suffer from the "curse of dimensionality." As the number of nodes |V| and links |E| scales, the state-action space size increases exponentially:

|S × A| ∝ K · (|V| · |E|)^2 This scaling behavior makes it impossible to maintain, store, or update tabular Q\-tables in enterprise-scale carrier networks.

### ***2.3.2 Deep Q-Networks and Double DQN in SDN***

To address the dimensionality constraints of tabular routing, researchers replaced the discrete lookup table with a deep neural network (DNN) that acts as a non-linear function approximator, Q(s,a;θ) ≈ Q\*(s,a). Casas-Velasco et al. (2021) proposed the Deep Reinforcement Learning and Software-Defined Networking Intelligent Routing (DRSIR) framework. DRSIR uses an off-policy DQN agent equipped with:

* **Experience Replay Memory (**D**):** Stores transition tuples (s\_t,a\_t,r\_t,s\_{t+1}) to break the temporal correlation of sequential network states.  
* **Target Network (**θ^-**):** Decouples the target value calculation from the online evaluation network () to stabilize training:

L(θ)=E\_{(s,a,r,s')∼D}\[(r \+ γ max\_{a'}Q(s',a';θ^-) \- Q(s,a;θ))^2\] DRSIR reduced traffic delay by 16.1% and improved throughput by 7.8% compared to standard OSPF. However, standard DQN architectures suffer from systematic overestimation of action-value functions. This is because the max\_a operator selects the maximum estimated value, propagating positive noise and sub-optimal routing path evaluations throughout training.

To resolve this, Zhang et al. (2024) applied Double DQN (DDQN) to SDN routing under dynamic traffic conditions. DDQN mitigates value overestimation by decoupling the selection of the maximizing action from its evaluation. The online network selects the action, while the target network evaluates its value:

L\_Double(θ)=E\_{(s,a,r,s')∼D}\[(r \+ γQ(s',arg max\_{a'}Q(s',a';θ);θ^-) \- Q(s,a;θ))^2\] Empirical evaluations showed that DDQN agents maintain higher throughput across fluctuating traffic patterns and achieve superior convergence stability compared to standard DQN.

### ***2.3.3 Actor-Critic and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)***

When routing requires fine-grained, continuous-like traffic engineering—such as dynamically adjusting OSPF link weights or determining bandwidth allocations—value-based methods are no longer suitable. Actor-Critic models use two neural networks: an Actor network (π\_θ(s,a)) that outputs actions, and a Critic network (V\_ϕ(s)) that estimates the state value function.

The Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) algorithm is one such Actor-Critic variant. However, it often suffers from severe training instability and high hyperparameter sensitivity in dynamic SDN environments (Abrol et al., 2024). This is because minor updates to the actor policy can significantly change routing paths across the network, leading to route flapping, congestion, and packet drops.

To address this instability, Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) uses a clipped surrogate objective function that restricts the policy update step:

L^{CLIP}(θ)= Ê\_t\[min(r\_t(θ)Â\_t, clip(r\_t(θ),1-ε,1+ε)Â\_t)\] Where the probability ratio r\_t(θ) is defined as:

r\_t(θ)=π\_θ(a\_t|s\_t)/π\_{θ\_old}(a\_t|s\_t) And Â\_t is the generalized advantage estimator computed by the Critic network. The clipping parameter  (typically 0.1 or 0.2) prevents the updated policy from deviating too far from the old policy. This constraint prevents the dramatic, catastrophic performance drops during exploration that often affect DDPG and DQN.

Consequently, PPO achieves faster convergence, more stable learning, and lower average latency in complex Fat-Tree topologies compared to both value-based and deterministic actor-critic baselines (Abrol et al., 2024).

## **2.4 Topological Representation and Generalization (Graph Neural Networks & Transformers)**

A significant challenge in deploying deep learning models to production environments is the dynamic nature of physical network topologies. Standard Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs), and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) require structured, fixed-dimension inputs.

### ***2.4.1 The Curse of Non-Euclidean Topologies***

Because computer networks are represented as non-Euclidean graphs, mapping the network state into a flat matrix introduces structural limitations:

1. **Fixed-size Input Constraint:** If a network link fails or a new switch node is added to the topology, the dimension of the State Space Matrix changes. For a traditional MLP-based DRL agent, even minor topological modifications change the input dimension, requiring the neural network to be redesigned and retrained from scratch (Wang et al., 2025).  
2. **Structural Blindness:** Flat matrices do not preserve the spatial and topological dependencies of the network. Traditional neural networks struggle to learn how changes in traffic load on a distant bottleneck link affect neighboring or alternate paths.

### ***2.4.2 Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) and GNNs in Routing***

To capture network topology structures, researchers integrated Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) into the DRL control loop. GNNs process graph-structured inputs directly using message-passing operations. At each layer l, node features h\_i^{(l)} and link features h\_{ij}^{(l)} are updated by aggregating state information from their local neighbors:

m\_i^{(l+1)} \= AGGREGATE^{(l)}({(h\_j^{(l)},h\_{ij}^{(l)}): v\_j ∈ N(v\_i)}) h\_i^{(l+1)} \= UPDATE^{(l)}(h\_i^{(l)},m\_i^{(l+1)}) Where N(v\_i) is the set of neighboring nodes adjacent to node v\_i. By learning from graph structures rather than fixed matrices, GNN-based DRL models can generalize to unseen topologies, allowing them to remain operational after link or node failures without requiring immediate retraining.

### ***2.4.3 Graph Transformers and Global Perception***

Although standard GNN-based Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) capture local topology details, they struggle with "over-smoothing" and "over-squashing" when propagating information across long distances in large networks (Iqbal & Anjum, 2026). Over-squashing occurs when an exponentially increasing amount of graph neighborhood data is compressed into fixed-size node embeddings, which can prevent the agent from detecting distant congestion bottlenecks.

To address these limitations, recent architectures deploy Graph Transformers. Wang et al. (2025) proposed the Graph Transformer Star Routing (GTSR) algorithm. GTSR uses a multi-head self-attention mechanism that integrates topological bias directly into the attention calculation:

A\_{ij}^{(h)} \= exp((q\_i^{(h)}(k\_j^{(h)})^T)/√d\_h \+ ψ(e\_{ij})) / ∑\_{u∈V} exp((q\_i^{(h)}(k\_u^{(h)})^T)/√d\_h \+ ψ(e\_{iu})) Where q\_i represents the query vector of node i, k\_j is the key vector of node j, and ψ(e\_{ij}) is a learned spatial embedding that represents the shortest-path distance or routing cost between node i and node j in the physical topology.

GTSR also introduces a virtual "star node" connected to all other nodes in the network graph. This virtual node provides a direct communication pathway across the entire network diameter, enabling global message aggregation in a single step.

Empirical results showed that GTSR can adapt to unseen topological changes without retraining, reducing end-to-end routing latency by 47% and packet loss rates by 10% compared to traditional GNN and non-graph baselines (Wang et al., 2025).

## **2.5 Safe Deep Reinforcement Learning and Network Digital Twins**

A major obstacle to deploying deep reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithms in production environments is the "reality gap" and the risks associated with the exploration phase.

### ***2.5.1 The "Reality Gap" and Exploration Hazards***

During the initial training phase, a DRL agent learns primarily through trial and error. In a live production network, selecting untested or random routing paths to explore the state space can cause severe packet loss, network-wide congestion, routing loops, and SLA violations. This behavior is unacceptable for enterprise or carrier-grade systems that require high availability and predictable QoS.

### ***2.5.2 Network Digital Twins***

To eliminate live-network degradation, modern frameworks train DRL agents in an offline Network Digital Twin that mirrors the performance of the physical data plane (Iqbal & Anjum, 2026). The digital twin acts as a high-fidelity, simulated sandbox that models network behavior using historical traffic matrices and queue analysis.

To provide accurate telemetry estimates, the digital twin models physical switches using M/M/1/K queuing theory. In this formulation:

* Packet arrivals at switch interface buffers follow a Poisson process with arrival rate .  
* Transmission and packet processing service times follow an exponential distribution with service rate .  
* Each switch port has a finite buffer capacity of K packets.

The traffic intensity  is defined as:

ρ \= λ/μ When ρ ≠ 1, the steady-state probability of having exactly n packets in the buffer is calculated as:

P\_n \= ((1-ρ)/(1-ρ^{K+1}))ρ^n,   0 ≤ n ≤ K The probability of a buffer overflow (which leads to packet loss) is the probability of the buffer being completely full (P\_K):

P\_overflow \= P\_K \= ((1-ρ)/(1-ρ^{K+1}))ρ^K The expected queue length (average packet occupancy) E\[Q\] at each switch interface is modeled as:

E\[Q\] \= ρ\[1-(K+1)ρ^K+Kρ^{K+1}\] / ((1-ρ)(1-ρ^{K+1})) By modeling these queuing equations across all ports, the Network Digital Twin can estimate latency, throughput, and packet loss for any routing policy computed by the DRL agent.

The agent can then train for millions of steps in this simulated sandbox, encountering rare congestion events and high-traffic scenarios without impacting the physical network. Once the policy converges and is validated, the trained model parameters are transferred to the live SDN controller.

## **2.6 The Confluence of Agentic AI and Intent-Based Networking (IBN)**

The integration of Agentic AI and Large Language Models (LLMs) represents a major advancement in autonomous network management. While DRL excels at low-level numerical path optimization, it lacks semantic reasoning, contextual understanding of business intents, and interpretability.

### ***2.6.1 Large Language Models (LLMs) in Network Orchestration***

Modern Intent-Based Networking (IBN) frameworks use LLMs to bridge the gap between high-level business policies and low-level network configurations. Cui et al. (2025) introduced TrafficLLM, a framework that uses fine-tuned LLMs to analyze raw network traffic and parse natural language operator commands.

This agentic layer allows operators to define network policies using high-level semantic intent, such as:

*"Prioritize video-conferencing traffic across the European network slice and minimize latency, while allowing file-transfer flows to take longer paths."*

The Agentic AI layer parses this command and translates the semantic intent into precise numerical reward weights (α\_1, α\_2, α\_3, α\_4) for the DRL agent's reward function. This dynamic reward adjustment aligns the DRL agent's routing decisions with active business requirements in real-time.

### ***2.6.2 Fault-Tolerance and Fallback Systems***

To ensure high availability, agentic routing frameworks include deterministic fallback systems to protect against DRL model failures or complex physical link outages. Bholani (2026) proposed the Self-Healing Router (SHR) architecture, which separates the control loop into two operational paths:

1. **Fast Path (Deterministic Fallback):** Runs traditional, highly reliable routing algorithms (e.g., Dijkstra or ECMP) directly on the controller.  
2. **Slow Path (Intelligent Orchestration):** Deploys the DRL/LLM orchestrator to calculate globally optimized routing pathways and adjust policy configurations.

If a severe link failure or network event occurs, the fast path takes over immediately, routing traffic along deterministic backup paths within microseconds. The computationally intensive DRL/LLM agent is then invoked to re-evaluate the global topology state, compute an optimized routing policy, and update the fast-path forwarding rules.

This hybrid approach reduces control-plane latency by over 90% during transient failures while maintaining stable and optimized routing performance.

## **2.7 Summary of Literature and Gap Analysis**

To contextualize the proposed Graph-Attention Reinforcement Routing Orchestrator (GARRO), Table 2.3 synthesizes existing literature across core technical dimensions.

| Reference | Core Methodology | Primary Objective | Key Achievements | Limitations & Gaps |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Casas-Velasco et al. (2021)** | DRSIR (DQN, Experience Replay) | Latency reduction, throughput optimization | 16.1% delay reduction, 7.8% throughput gain | Uses a fixed-dimension state representation; struggles with overestimation bias; unable to adapt to topology changes without retraining. |
| **Kim et al. (2022)** | Deep RL (DQN-based routing) | Minimizing path collisions | Improved multi-path routing compared to ECMP | Slow convergence; restricted to static, small-scale topologies; lacks safe exploration guarantees. |
| **Chen et al. (2024)** | AVRO (African Vulture Metaheuristic) | Bottleneck avoidance via centrality analysis | 16.9% improvement over standard DRL | High computational overhead; cannot make real-time, millisecond-level routing decisions during sudden traffic bursts. |
| **Zhang et al. (2024)** | Hybrid DRL (Double DQN / DDQN) | Convergence stability in dynamic traffic | Stable policy learning under varying loads | Discrete action limits; does not support continuous weight tuning; vulnerable to exploration risks in production. |
| **Abrol et al. (2024)** | PPO with Graph Convolutional Networks | Adaptive routing in next-gen topologies | Stable policy learning; reduced latency in Fat-Tree networks | Message aggregation is limited to local neighborhoods; prone to over-squashing in large WAN topologies. |
| **Wang et al. (2025)** | GTSR (Graph Transformer Star Routing) | Robustness against topological failures | Zero-shot generalization; 47% reduction in latency | Lacks integration with high-level operational intent and human-in-the-loop validation layers. |
| **Cui et al. (2025)** | TrafficLLM (Fine-tuned LLM) | Semantic intent translation, traffic analysis | Natural language network policy execution | High computational cost of LLM inference; lacks real-time, microsecond-level path optimization. |
| **Bholani (2026)** | Self-Healing Router (Fast/Slow hybrid) | Fault tolerance, recovery acceleration | 90% control-plane latency reduction during failure | Relies on traditional heuristics for fallback; does not use topological graph neural networks. |
| **Iqbal & Anjum (2026)** | Network Digital Twin (Graph MPNNs) | Congestion-aware predictive routing | Accurate, safe offline simulation mapping | Relies on local message-passing GNNs; struggles to scale in highly asymmetric networks. |

*Table 2.3: Systematic Synthesis of State-of-the-Art Literature.*

### ***2.7.1 The Identified Research Gap***

The systematic review of the literature reveals a clear research gap in autonomous routing systems:

1. **Isolation of Topology and Safety:** Existing robust routing models (like GTSR) solve topological agility but ignore the safety hazards of live exploration. Conversely, digital twin and safe DRL frameworks (like Iqbal & Anjum, 2026\) rely on local message-passing GNNs that struggle to scale in large, asymmetric topologies.  
2. **Lack of Intent Integration:** Current DRL frameworks rely on fixed reward coefficients (), making them unable to adapt dynamically to changing business goals or slice-specific SLAs without manual reconfiguration. Meanwhile, LLM-based systems (like TrafficLLM) lack the execution speed required for microsecond-level packet forwarding.  
3. **Absence of an Integrated Framework:** No existing work combines the global perception of **Graph Transformers**, the training stability of **PPO**, the safety of a **Digital Twin**, and the semantic orchestration of **Agentic AI** into a single, cohesive routing engine.

### ***2.7.2 The GARRO Proposal***

The Graph-Attention Reinforcement Routing Orchestrator (GARRO) is designed to address this research gap by integrating these advanced capabilities into a unified architecture:

* **Topological Agility:** Uses a Graph Transformer with a virtual star node to provide global perception and zero-shot generalization across dynamic topologies.  
* **Stable & Efficient Learning:** Deploys a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent to ensure stable policy convergence and prevent route flapping.  
* **Risk-Free Training:** Uses an offline Network Digital Twin modeled with M/M/1/K queuing theory to eliminate live-network exploration risks.  
* **Intent-Based Orchestration:** Integrates an Agentic AI layer to dynamically translate high-level operational commands into DRL reward parameters and provide deterministic, fault-tolerant fallback mechanisms.

# 

# **3.0 Methodology**

## **3.1 Introduction**

This chapter outlines the methodology and architectural implementation strategy for the proposed Graph-Attention Reinforcement Routing Orchestrator (GARRO). The primary objective of this experimental research is to design, simulate, and evaluate a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agent integrated with Graph Transformers and an Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI) layer for dynamic Software-Defined Networking (SDN) routing.

To conduct a rigorous, replicable evaluation without requiring enterprise-grade physical hardware, the methodology relies on a high-fidelity simulation and emulation testbed. The system decouples the intense computational requirements of the AI Decision Plane from the real-time event loops of the SDN Control Plane, utilizing cloud-based Large Language Model (LLM) APIs to provide semantic reasoning capabilities within local hardware constraints.

## **3.2 Experimental Environment and Toolchain**

Given the reliance on native Linux networking namespaces for accurate data plane emulation, the environment will be hosted on a Windows machine utilizing the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL). WSL provides a seamless, native Ubuntu Linux kernel environment capable of running the required network emulators while maintaining the convenience of the host Windows operating system.

The software stack and toolchain are defined as follows:

* **Operating System Environment:** Windows 11 running WSL 2 (Ubuntu 22.04 LTS).  
* **Programming Language:** Python 3.10+, selected for its extensive support across both SDN controller development and deep learning frameworks.  
* **Data Plane Emulator:** Mininet. Mininet uses Linux network namespaces to create a realistic virtual network, allowing for the deployment of OpenFlow-enabled virtual switches (Open vSwitch) and virtual hosts.  
* **SDN Controller (Control Plane):** OS-Ken. OS-Ken is a component-based software-defined networking framework built in Python. It provides out-of-the-box support for OpenFlow 1.3, asynchronous event handling, and a RESTful API architecture.  
* **AI and Machine Learning Framework:** PyTorch will be used to construct the Graph Transformer encoder and the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent, complemented by Ray RLlib for distributed reinforcement learning training capabilities.  
* **Agentic AI Engine:** Due to the hardware constraints of running multi-billion parameter LLMs locally, the Agentic AI layer will integrate external RESTful APIs (either Google Gemini API or OpenAI GPT-4 API). This approach offloads semantic processing to the cloud.

## 

## 

## **3.3 System Architecture Implementation**

The GARRO architecture is implemented using a highly decoupled, three-plane design to prevent the heavy computational loads of the AI models from blocking the asynchronous network event loops of the OS-Ken controller.

### ***3.3.1 Data Plane Implementation (Mininet)***

The physical network is emulated in Mininet using Open vSwitch (OVS) instances configured to communicate via the OpenFlow 1.3 protocol. Mininet handles the creation of the underlying topologies (NSFNET, GEANT2, Fat-Tree) and enforces link characteristics such as maximum bandwidth capacities, propagation delays, and queue limits. Traffic generation across this plane is executed using iperf3 to simulate continuous flows, alongside the Modulated Gravity Model (TMGen) to introduce highly bursty, non-stationary microburst traffic characteristics typical of modern IoT and multimedia workloads.

### ***3.3.2 Control Plane Implementation (OS-Ken)***

The OS-Ken controller acts as the intermediary between the network and the AI. It is implemented via two distinct functional modules:

1. **Topology and Telemetry Discovery Module:** This module utilizes the Link Layer Discovery Protocol (LLDP) to map the dynamic network graph. It actively polls the Open vSwitch instances at fixed intervals (e.g., every 1–3 seconds) using OFPPortStatsRequest and OFPFlowStatsRequest messages to gather real-time telemetry, including byte counts, transmission rates, port errors, and active queue depths.  
2. **Northbound REST API Gateway:** To prevent PyTorch training loops from blocking OS-Ken’s core event thread, OS-Ken exposes a Northbound REST API via its WSGI web server functionality. The gathered network state is formatted into a JSON payload and served to the AI plane, while computed routing paths (flow rules) are received via POST requests and converted into OFPFlowMod messages pushed down to the data plane.

### ***3.3.3 AI Decision Plane Implementation***

The AI plane operates as a standalone Python process interacting with Ryu via the REST API. It consists of three tightly integrated components:

**1\. Graph Transformer Encoder:**

The JSON telemetry from OS-Ken is converted into a PyTorch Geometric (PyG) graph data object. Each switch is treated as a node containing features (CPU, buffer state), and each physical connection is an edge (throughput, latency, capacity). The Graph Transformer utilizes multi-head self-attention with a virtual star node to compress this variable-sized network topology into a fixed-size latent state vector, ensuring the DRL agent can process the network state even if a link dynamically fails.

**2\. PPO Decision Engine:**

The latent state vector is ingested by the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent. PPO utilizes an Actor-Critic neural network architecture. The Actor network outputs a probability distribution over a set of K\-shortest paths (pre-computed via Yen's algorithm) for a given source-destination pair, selecting the path that optimally balances load and minimizes latency. The objective function clipping in PPO ensures that policy updates do not deviate drastically, maintaining routing stability.

**3\. Agentic AI Layer (LLM API Integration):**

The agentic layer utilizes the Gemini or OpenAI API to parse human intent. Network operators input high-level natural language commands (e.g., *"Ensure ultra-low latency for Video Conferencing flows, throughput is secondary"*). The Python backend packages this prompt with context and sends it to the LLM API. The LLM is prompt-engineered to return a structured JSON response containing normalized floating-point weights for the DRL reward function variables: α\_1 (Throughput), α\_2 (Delay), α\_3 (Packet Loss), and α\_4 (Link Variance). This effectively allows human language to dynamically reprogram the mathematical objectives of the PPO agent in real-time.

## **3.4 Operational Flow and Digital Twin Training Regimen**

Deploying an untrained PPO agent directly into the Mininet-OS-Ken emulation would lead to chaotic exploration, causing massive packet drops and routing loops (the "reality gap"). To counteract this, GARRO implements a two-phase training methodology.

### ***Phase 1: Offline Digital Twin Simulation***

An offline digital twin environment is programmed purely in Python, bypassing Mininet and OS-Ken entirely. The network is modeled mathematically using M/M/1/K queuing theory. The digital twin simulates traffic arrivals as a Poisson process and service times exponentially.

* **State Generation:** The twin algorithmically generates millions of randomized network states and traffic matrices.  
* **Exploration:** The PPO agent explores routing actions rapidly without real-world time constraints. Reward feedback is calculated using the mathematically expected queue depths and overflow probabilities. The model iterates until policy convergence is achieved for standard traffic patterns.

### ***Phase 2: Live Emulation and Online Adaptation***

Once the offline policy converges, the pre-trained weights are loaded into the PyTorch model connected to the live OS-Ken-Mininet testbed.

* **State Retrieval:** The Python AI orchestrator queries OS-Ken's REST API for the live Network State Matrix.  
* **Action Execution:** The PPO agent, now safely initialized, selects optimal paths.  
* **Flow Installation:** The orchestrator POSTs the chosen path to OS-Ken, which installs the specific MATCH (Source IP, Dest IP, Port) and ACTION (Forward out port X) rules in the Open vSwitch flow tables.  
* **Continuous Learning:** The agent continues to refine its policy using real-time delayed reward signals gathered from OS-Ken's ongoing telemetry polling.

## **3.5 Experimental Setup and Evaluation Parameters**

The evaluation will be conducted across three distinct network topologies deployed within Mininet to validate the model's scalability and graph-generalization capabilities:

1. **NSFNET (14 nodes, 21 links):** Evaluates performance on a standard, sparse Wide Area Network (WAN) backbone.  
2. **GEANT2 (24 nodes, 37 links):** Evaluates load-balancing efficiency across a highly irregular and asymmetrical European academic network topology.  
3. **Fat-Tree (k=8, 80 nodes):** Assesses the algorithm's collision avoidance and multi-path optimization capabilities in dense, data-center architectures characterized by heavy East-West traffic.

### ***3.5.1 Baseline Algorithms for Comparison***

To accurately gauge the performance enhancements introduced by GARRO, the framework will be benchmarked against:

* **Open Shortest Path First (OSPF):** Standard shortest-path routing based on fixed hop counts/weights (implemented via OS-Ken routing modules).  
* **Equal-Cost Multi-Path (ECMP):** Hash-based flow splitting across multiple equal-cost paths.  
* **Standard Deep Q-Network (DQN):** A value-based DRL baseline to prove the superior stability of the PPO and Graph Transformer approach.

### ***3.5.2 Performance Metrics***

The system's efficacy will be quantitatively measured using the following Quality of Service (QoS) metrics:

1. **End-to-End Latency (ms):** The average time taken for packets to traverse from source to destination.  
2. **Network Throughput (Mbps):** The total successful data transfer rate, evaluating the agent's ability to utilize parallel link bandwidths without causing congestion.  
3. **Packet Loss Ratio (%):** The percentage of packets dropped due to queue buffer overflows at bottleneck links.  
4. **Link Utilization Variance:** The statistical variance in bandwidth usage across all network links. A lower variance indicates a highly optimized, balanced network where edge links are utilized effectively alongside central backbone links.  
5. **Control-Plane Overhead:** Measured by the latency between a "Packet-In" event at the OS-Ken controller and the corresponding "Flow-Mod" execution, evaluating if the Python-REST-OS-Ken architecture introduces unmanageable signaling delays.

**References**

Abrol, A., Mohan, P. M., & Truong-Huu, T. (2024). *A deep reinforcement learning approach for adaptive traffic routing in next-gen networks.* arXiv. [https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04515](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04515)

Al-Sadi, A., & Al-Anbaki, H. (2025). An implementation of deep reinforcement learning-based routing framework for Open-Network Operating System-controlled and Mininet-emulated Software-Defined Networking. *IET Networks*, 14(1), 12–28. https://doi.org/10.1049/ntw2.70016

Bholani, N. (2026). *Self-healing router: Fault-tolerant orchestration architecture.* arXiv. https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01548

Casas-Velasco, D. M., Rendon, O. M. C., & da Fonseca, N. L. S. (2021). DRSIR: A deep reinforcement learning approach for routing in software-defined networking. *IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management, 18*(4), 4436–4450. https://doi.org/10.1109/TNSM.2021.3109864

Chen, J., Xiao, W., Zhang, H., Zuo, J., & Li, X. (2024). Dynamic routing optimization in software-defined networking based on a metaheuristic algorithm. *Journal of Cloud Computing, 13*(41). https://doi.org/10.1186/s13677-024-00601-3

Cui, T., Guo, J., Li, Z., Wu, Z., Zhang, H., Han, Z., & Wu, C. (2025). *TrafficLLM: Enhancing large language models for network traffic analysis with generic traffic representation.* arXiv. https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.04222

Goteti, D., & Reddy, V. K. (2026). Q-Optimizer: An AI-based optimization framework for efficient SDN routing and QoS enhancement. *Journal of Computer Science, 22*(1), 130–146. https://doi.org/10.3844/jcssp.2026.130.146

Kim, G., Kim, Y., & Lim, H. (2022). Deep reinforcement learning-based routing on software-defined networks. *IEEE Access, 10*, 18121–18133. https://doi.org/10.1109/ACCESS.2022.3150437

Zhang, Z., Guan, L., & Meng, Q. (2024). A hybrid deep reinforcement learning routing method under dynamic and complex traffic with software defined networking. *Neural Computing and Applications, 36*, 7231–7246. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00521-024-09438-0
